next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

The spoons began at the Ravenclaw table.

At first it sounded like somebody dropping cutlery one piece at a time: a light dry knock, then another, then a run of them. Owen looked up from the margin where he had been copying one of Prue's star numbers beside a line of Grinlow symbols and saw every spoon on the table rise an inch from the wood. They turned together, bowls gleaming with gravy and lamplight, and pointed straight at the High Table.

For one stupid second the Hall held still around it. Steam lifted from potatoes. A first-year at the end of Hufflepuff froze with custard on her chin and her mouth open. Then the spoons started hammering themselves against the rims of the serving bowls, not random, not noisy enough to be accidental. Three beats, pause. Three beats, pause. They all faced the teachers while they did it, as if a hundred bits of silver had developed opinions.

Amycus Carrow came up out of his chair so hard the bench scraped stone.

"Enough of that," he barked, and half the spoons dropped dead at once. The other half kept going for one more beat, stubborn as teeth chattering in a skull. Amycus drew his wand. The doors of the Hall slammed shut on the bang of it.

Owen's first thought was not fear. It was annoyance, pure and mean. He had one good hour before curfew. He had a copied line in the margin of his Charms essay, a prepared chalk stroke wrapped in paper inside his sleeve, and a decent chance of finding out whether the new surfaced table meant contact in the physical sense or only in the legal one. Somebody had decided to be brave with s...
open_hooks_to_carry: Prue proves the footnote survived from a censored print lineage and therefore cannot be dismissed as Owen's panic.
Give the chapter genuine stillness: food, cold, boredom, and mundane talk not immediately instrumentalized.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...to something he did not have the steadiness for. It was still faintly warm in the middle. He sat with it in one hand and the other wrapped under the table in cooling pain while homework resumed around them in soft scratchings and mutters.

After a minute Martha picked up her sock again.

"Green silk was all I had," she said, not looking at him.

"It suits the sock."

"Shut up."

That, at least, came out right.

Later, in the dormitory, he tried no more spells. He hid the manuscript under the false bottom he had made in his trunk from a cracked Charms text and two strips of packing wood. He tied the trunk shut by hand instead of trusting the latch. Then he sat on the edge of the bed with the wand across his knees and unwrapped the bandage.

The new lines in his palm had darkened. They no longer looked like the random after-effects of damaged skin. They looked appended.

He turned the wand once between thumb and fingers. Ordinary magic had always felt, if not easy, then legible. You learned the angle, the emphasis, the bit of language that made the world agree to a small temporary impossibility. Tonight the wand felt like a schoolmate who had seen him do something shameful and had not decided whether to keep speaking to him.

He had wanted proof the Grinlow Leaves were more than a hidden old book full of lunatic marginalia.

He had it now in the lashed-up greenhouse with its tied doors and humiliated cupboards, in Martha's thread tin springing open at the wrong word, and in the hot added lines crossing his hand as though the manuscript had begun making notes on him in return.
state_deltas_required: Secrecy now conflicts with Owen's need for another mind able to parse the manuscript's censored references.
Owen lets Prue into the work, and together they surface the first ward tables.
